perturbation.original_text: When informed of the proprietary and confidential nature of Confidential Information that has been disclosed by the other Party, the receiving Party (“Recipient”) shall, for a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure, refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to any contractor or other third party without prior, written approval from the disclosing Party and shall protect such Confidential Information from inadvertent disclosure to a third party using the same care and diligence that the Recipient uses to protect its own proprietary and confidential information, but in no case less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: When informed of the proprietary and confidential nature of Confidential Information that has been disclosed by the other Party, the receiving Party (“Recipient”) shall, for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure, refrain from disclosing such Confidential Information to any contractor or other third party without prior, written approval from the disclosing Party and shall protect such Confidential Information from inadvertent disclosure to a third party using the same care and diligence that the Recipient uses to protect its own proprietary and confidential information, but in no case less than reasonable care.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the confidentiality period to one year may be too short for certain types of trade secrets, potentially contradicting laws protecting trade secrets for a 'reasonable period' which is usually longer.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets, which are often confidential business information. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer lifespan. A court might find that one year is not a reasonable duration to prevent misappropriation of a trade secret, which contradicts the underlying principles of trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The change from a three-year to a one-year confidentiality period creates a contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation states the modified period 'may be too short for certain types of trade secrets, potentially contradicting laws protecting trade secrets for a reasonable period', implying the one year may not adequately protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation supports this further, stating that a 'one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to protect trade secrets, especially if the information has a longer lifespan' and 'A court might find that one year is not a reasonable duration to prevent misappropriation of a trade secret.' Although there were no applicable scraped law snippets, the combined explanations strongly suggest that the modified contract term would be legally challenged.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall promptly notify the disclosing Party of any disclosure of such Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement or of any subpoena or other legal process requiring production or disclosure of said Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient of Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall notify the disclosing Party of any disclosure of such Confidential Information in violation of this Agreement or of any subpoena or other legal process requiring production or disclosure of said Confidential Information within 30 days of discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification to 30 days could violate regulations that require immediate reporting of data breaches or security incidents under various data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Various State Data Breach Notification Laws (e.g., California Consumer Privacy Act - CCPA)
perturbation.law_explanation: CCPA (and other state data breach laws) often require businesses to provide notice of a data breach 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay.' A 30-day delay could be deemed an 'unreasonable delay,' thus violating the prompt notification requirements of data breach laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day delay in notifying the disclosing party of a data breach. This contradicts the legal requirement for prompt notification as required by data breach laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 1798.29 (a), mandates disclosure of any security breach 'following discovery or notification of the breach.' While it doesn't explicitly define 'following', the generally accepted legal interpretation is 'without unreasonable delay'. A 30-day delay can be considered an unreasonable delay. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation also highlights this potential violation of prompt notification requirements. The modification creates a legal inconsistency making the changed contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that the disclosing Party will suffer irreparable injury if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the disclosing Party shall only be entitled to monetary damages, not injunctive relief, against a threatened breach or continuation of any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to injunctive relief could be seen as an unenforceable limitation of remedies, especially in cases involving trade secrets where injunctive relief is crucial.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Availability of Injunctive Relief for Trade Secret Misappropriation under UTSA
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the UTSA, injunctive relief is a standard remedy for trade secret misappropriation. Contractually removing the right to seek injunctive relief is against the spirit and intent of trade secret law, as monetary damages are often inadequate to compensate for the loss of competitive advantage resulting from the disclosure of trade secrets. A court might not enforce such a limitation, particularly where irreparable harm is evident.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the disclosing party's right to injunctive relief, limiting them to only monetary damages. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both point out that injunctive relief is a standard remedy for trade secret misappropriation, and that monetary damages alone may be inadequate. Although the provided snippets are unhelpful, the removal of this remedy directly contradicts the purpose of trade secret law, as it weakens the ability to prevent irreparable harm, creating a legal contradiction that could make the changed clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of three (3) years from the Effective Date unless otherwise terminated by either Party giving notice to the other of its desire to terminate this Agreement. The requirement to protect Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect until terminated by either Party giving 30 days notice to the other. Upon termination, all confidentiality obligations cease immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Ending all confidentiality obligations upon termination, regardless of the information's nature or the circumstances, could violate trade secret laws if it allows for the immediate disclosure of information that still qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret protection can last indefinitely, as long as the information remains a secret and provides a competitive advantage. Ending confidentiality obligations immediately upon termination, without regard to whether the information still qualifies as a trade secret, would contradict the ongoing protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction because it eliminates confidentiality obligations immediately upon termination of the agreement, which the Law Explanation states contradicts the ongoing protection afforded to trade secrets under the UTSA, as trade secret protection can last indefinitely. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation and Law explanations both indicate a violation of trade secret laws.
